preamble.summary: After Saul's death on Mount Gilboa, an Amalekite messenger arrives at David's camp in Ziklag claiming to have delivered the killing blow to the wounded king. Rather than rewarding him, David has the man executed for daring to strike the LORD's anointed. David then composes a formal lament over Saul and Jonathan — the Song of the Bow — one of the most celebrated poems in the Hebrew Bible. Its refrain, 'How the mighty have fallen,' echoes three times as David mourns the loss of Israel's king and his own covenant brother.
preamble.remarkable: This chapter contains the oldest securely datable poem in the Davidic corpus. The Song of the Bow (vv. 19-27) is a qinah, a formal lament composed in the distinctive 3:2 'limping meter' that Hebrew poets reserved for dirges — the longer first line followed by the shorter second line creates a rhythmic stumbling, as if the verse itself is collapsing under the weight of grief. David orders it taught to the people of Judah and attributes it to the Book of Jashar, an ancient collection of heroic poetry now lost. The poem contains no theology: there is no mention of God, no appeal to divine justice, no comfort of resurrection. It is pure grief rendered as art. The Amalekite's story in verses 6-10 contradicts the account of Saul's death in 1 Samuel 31:4, where Saul falls on his own sword. Whether the Amalekite lied to curry favor or whether both accounts preserve different traditions, the narrator does not resolve — but David's response is unambiguous: to claim credit for killing the LORD's anointed is a capital offense regardless of the circumstances.
preamble.friction: The central friction is the discrepancy between 1 Samuel 31:4 (Saul fell on his own sword) and the Amalekite's account here (he delivered the final blow at Saul's request). Three interpretive options exist: (1) the Amalekite fabricated the story hoping for reward, (2) both accounts are partially true — Saul fell on his sword but did not die immediately, and the Amalekite finished the act, or (3) the two sources preserve rival traditions. We render the Amalekite's speech straightforwardly and let the translator's notes address the tension. A second friction point lies in verse 18, where the Hebrew says 'He said to teach the sons of Judah the bow' — the word qeshet ('bow') appears to be a title for the lament, but its abrupt insertion is syntactically awkward. Some manuscripts and the Septuagint omit it or rearrange. We retain 'the Bow' as the poem's title, following the Masoretic text. Verse 26 presents a third tension: David's declaration that Jonathan's love surpassed 'the love of women' (ahavat nashim) has generated extensive debate about its meaning. The Hebrew ahavah encompasses the full range of covenantal loyalty, political alliance, deep friendship, and emotional attachment. We render it faithfully and address the semantic range in the key terms.
preamble.connections: The Song of the Bow connects backward to the Jabesh-gilead rescue of Saul's body (1 Samuel 31:11-13) and forward to David's honoring of those same men (2 Samuel 2:5-7). David's insistence on the sanctity of the LORD's anointed (mashiach YHWH) in verse 14 echoes his repeated refusal to harm Saul in 1 Samuel 24:6 and 26:9-11 — the theological principle that governed his fugitive years now governs his response to Saul's death. The reference to the Book of Jashar (v. 18) connects to Joshua 10:13, where the same source is cited for Joshua's command to the sun. The 'mighty' (gibborim) who have fallen will become a key category in David's kingdom — his elite warriors are called gibborim (2 Samuel 23:8-39). Jonathan's bow, celebrated in verse 22, recalls the covenant gift of his bow to David in 1 Samuel 18:4. The chapter's geographic movement — from Gilboa to Ziklag — bridges the Saul narrative and the David narrative, placing the reader at the threshold of David's rise to kingship.

verses.text_hebrew: וַיְהִ֗י אַחֲרֵ֛י מ֥וֹת שָׁא֖וּל וְדָוִ֣ד שָׁ֑ב מֵהַכּ֣וֹת אֶת־הָעֲמָלֵ֔ק וַיֵּ֧שֶׁב דָּוִ֛ד בְּצִקְלַ֖ג יָמִ֥ים שְׁנָֽיִם׃
verses.text_kjv: Now it came to pass after the death of Saul, when David was returned from the slaughter of the Amalekites, and David had abode two days in Ziklag;
verses.rendering: After the death of Saul, David had returned from striking down the Amalekites and stayed in Ziklag for two days.
verses.expanded_rendering: The opening clause acharei mot Sha'ul ('after the death of Saul') functions as a temporal bridge connecting this book to the final chapter of 1 Samuel. The narrator situates David geographically and temporally: he is in Ziklag, the Philistine-granted town (1 Samuel 27:6), fresh from his retaliatory raid against the Amalekites who had raided and burned the town (1 Samuel 30). The detail 'two days' (yamim shenayim) places David in a specific window of ignorance — he does not yet know what has happened on Gilboa. The irony is acute: David has just defeated Amalekites, and it will be an Amalekite who brings him the news of Saul's death.
translator_notes: The phrase mehakkot et ha-Amaleq ('from striking the Amalekites') uses the Hiphil infinitive of nakah, which denotes a decisive military blow — David did not merely skirmish with the Amalekites but struck them decisively. This is the same campaign narrated in 1 Samuel 30.

Ziklag's status is ambiguous: it was a Philistine city given to David by Achish of Gath (1 Samuel 27:6), making David technically a Philistine vassal at this moment. The narrator does not comment on this political awkwardness. The 'two days' of waiting create narrative tension — the reader already knows Saul is dead, but David does not.

verses.text_hebrew: וַיְהִ֣י ׀ בַּיּ֣וֹם הַשְּׁלִישִׁ֗י וְהִנֵּה֩ אִ֨ישׁ בָּ֤א מִן־הַֽמַּחֲנֶה֙ מֵעִ֣ם שָׁא֔וּל וּבְגָדָ֣יו קְרֻעִ֔ים וַאֲדָמָ֖ה עַל־רֹאשׁ֑וֹ וַיְהִי֙ בְּבֹא֣וֹ אֶל־דָּוִ֔ד וַיִּפֹּ֥ל אַ֖רְצָה וַיִּשְׁתָּֽחוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: It came even to pass on the third day, that, behold, a man came out of the camp from Saul with his clothes rent, and earth upon his head: and so it was, when he came to David, that he fell to the earth, and did obeisance.
verses.rendering: On the third day, a man arrived from Saul's camp with his clothes torn and dirt on his head. When he came before David, he fell to the ground and bowed low.
verses.expanded_rendering: The third day (yom ha-shelishi) carries weight in Hebrew narrative — it is the day of resolution, the day things come to a head (Genesis 22:4, Exodus 19:11, Hosea 6:2). The man's appearance broadcasts catastrophe before he speaks a word: torn garments (begadav qeru'im) and earth on his head (adamah al rosho) are the two classic physical signs of mourning and disaster in ancient Israel (Joshua 7:6, 1 Samuel 4:12). The verbs at the end — naphal artsah ('he fell to the ground') and vayyishtachu ('he bowed low') — mark extreme deference, the posture of a subject before a king. This is significant: the man treats David as royalty before David holds any royal title.
translator_notes: The description of torn clothes and dirt on the head exactly mirrors the messenger who brought news of the ark's capture in 1 Samuel 4:12. The narrative pattern signals that another national disaster is being reported. The two scenes share the same vocabulary of catastrophe.

The verb vayyishtachu ('he prostrated himself') is the Hishtaphel of chavah, the most intense form of physical reverence in Hebrew. The man's posture toward David is politically charged: he is approaching David as one approaches a sovereign, possibly calculating that David will now claim the throne and reward anyone who facilitated the transition.

verses.text_hebrew: וַיֹּ֤אמֶר לוֹ֙ דָּוִ֔ד אֵ֥י מִזֶּ֖ה תָּב֑וֹא וַיֹּ֣אמֶר אֵלָ֔יו מִמַּחֲנֵ֥ה יִשְׂרָאֵ֖ל נִמְלָֽטְתִּי׃
verses.text_kjv: And David said unto him, From whence comest thou? And he said unto him, Out of the camp of Israel am I escaped.
verses.rendering: David asked him, "Where have you come from?" He answered, "I escaped from the camp of Israel."
verses.expanded_rendering: David's question ei mizzeh tavo ('where from this do you come') is a standard interrogative, but the man's answer carries more than geography. The verb nimlateti ('I escaped') is a Niphal perfect of malat, meaning to slip away, to deliver oneself from danger. It implies that the camp of Israel was a place from which one must escape — a site of defeat, not a functioning military position. The word machaneh ('camp') is the same term used for organized military encampments throughout the Saul narratives, but the man's flight from it signals that the camp has collapsed.
translator_notes: The verb malat in the Niphal implies narrow escape from mortal danger. The same verb is used of David's escapes from Saul (1 Samuel 19:10, 19:17). The man presents himself as a survivor of catastrophe, establishing credibility before delivering his report.

verses.text_hebrew: וַיֹּ֨אמֶר אֵלָ֥יו דָּוִ֛ד מֶה־הָיָ֥ה הַדָּבָ֖ר הַגֶּד־נָ֣א לִ֑י וַיֹּ֗אמֶר אֲשֶׁר־נָ֤ס הָעָם֙ מִן־הַמִּלְחָמָ֔ה וְגַם־הַרְבֵּ֞ה נָפְל֤וּ מִן־הָעָם֙ וַיָּמֻ֔תוּ וְגַ֗ם שָׁא֛וּל וִיהוֹנָתָ֥ן בְּנ֖וֹ מֵֽתוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: And David said unto him, How went the matter? I pray thee, tell me. And he answered, That the people are fled from the battle, and many of the people also are fallen and dead; and Saul and Jonathan his son are dead also.
verses.rendering: David said to him, "What happened? Tell me." He said, "The army fled from the battle, and many of the troops fell dead. And Saul and his son Jonathan are dead as well."
verses.expanded_rendering: David's demand meh hayah ha-davar ('what was the matter') presses for the substance behind the visual signs of mourning. The messenger's report follows a descending structure of catastrophe: first the army fled (nas ha-am, using the same verb for flight as in 1 Samuel 31:1), then many fell and died (harbeh nafelu min ha-am vayyamutu — the 'many' intensifies the disaster beyond ordinary battle losses), and finally the climactic blow: Saul and Jonathan are dead (metu). The report saves the worst for last. The pairing of Saul and Jonathan in death mirrors their pairing in David's lament (v. 23): 'In life and in death they were not divided.'
translator_notes: The structure of the report is rhetorically deliberate: general flight, mass casualties, then the specific royal deaths. Each clause escalates the disaster. The verb nas ('fled') signals complete rout, not orderly retreat. The phrase gam Sha'ul vi-Yehonatan beno metu ('also Saul and Jonathan his son died') delivers the two deaths in a single clause, binding father and son together in the messenger's grammar as they were bound on the battlefield.

verses.text_hebrew: וַיֹּ֣אמֶר דָּוִ֗ד אֶל־הַנַּ֙עַר֙ הַמַּגִּ֣יד ל֔וֹ אֵ֣יךְ יָדַ֔עְתָּ כִּי־מֵ֖ת שָׁא֣וּל וִיהוֹנָתָ֥ן בְּנֽוֹ׃
verses.text_kjv: And David said unto the young man that told him, How knowest thou that Saul and Jonathan his son be dead?
verses.rendering: David asked the young man who had brought the report, "How do you know that Saul and his son Jonathan are dead?"
verses.expanded_rendering: David addresses the messenger as ha-na'ar ha-maggid lo ('the young man who was telling him'). The word na'ar can range from 'boy' to 'young soldier' to 'attendant' — it marks the messenger as subordinate in both age and status. David's question eikh yadata ('how did you come to know') demands eyewitness evidence, not hearsay. The verb yada ('to know') in this context requires direct, experiential knowledge. David is cross-examining the messenger: the claim of royal deaths requires proof.
translator_notes: The term na'ar is deliberately vague regarding the messenger's exact social position. He could be a battlefield attendant, a young soldier, or even a camp follower. His identity as an Amalekite will emerge only later (v. 8), and the narrator withholds this detail to build suspense.

verses.text_hebrew: וַיֹּ֜אמֶר הַנַּ֣עַר הַמַּגִּ֣יד ל֗וֹ נִקְרֹ֤א נִקְרֵ֙יתִי֙ בְּהַ֣ר הַגִּלְבֹּ֔עַ וְהִנֵּ֣ה שָׁא֔וּל נִשְׁעָ֖ן עַל־חֲנִית֑וֹ וְהִנֵּ֥ה הָרֶ֛כֶב וּבַעֲלֵ֥י הַפָּרָשִׁ֖ים הִדְבִּקֻֽהוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: And the young man that told him said, As I happened by chance upon mount Gilboa, behold, Saul leaned upon his spear; and, lo, the chariots and horsemen followed hard after him.
verses.rendering: The young man reporting to him said, "I happened to be on Mount Gilboa, and there was Saul, leaning on his spear, with the chariots and cavalry closing in on him."
verses.expanded_rendering: The phrase niqro niqreiti ('happening, I happened') uses the infinitive absolute for emphasis — the messenger insists his presence was accidental, not deliberate. The image of Saul leaning on his spear (nish'an al chanito) is haunting: the spear has been Saul's signature weapon throughout the narrative (he hurled it at David in 1 Samuel 18:11, 19:10; he planted it by his head in 1 Samuel 26:7), and now he leans on it as a crutch, using the weapon of his aggression as a support for his broken body. The verb hidbi-quhu ('they were closing in on him') is the same davaq ('to cling, pursue closely') used in 1 Samuel 31:2, confirming the Amalekite's account matches the battlefield reality at least in its broad strokes.
translator_notes: The construction niqro niqreiti is the Niphal infinitive absolute plus finite verb of qarah ('to encounter, to happen upon'). The messenger frames his presence as pure chance, perhaps to deflect suspicion that he was looting the battlefield. Mount Gilboa is named, anchoring the account to the same location described in 1 Samuel 31.

The word chanit ('spear') rather than cherev ('sword') differs from the account in 1 Samuel 31:4, where Saul falls on his sword. This may support the interpretation that the Amalekite is fabricating or embellishing, or it may indicate a different moment in the sequence of events. The chariots (rekhev) and horsemen (ba'alei ha-parashim) represent the Philistine heavy forces — exactly the kind of units that would dominate in the open terrain near Gilboa.

verses.text_hebrew: וַיִּ֥פֶן אַחֲרָ֖יו וַיִּרְאֵ֑נִי וַיִּקְרָ֖א אֵלָֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: And when he looked behind him, he saw me, and called unto me.
verses.rendering: He turned and looked behind him, saw me, and called out to me.
verses.expanded_rendering: The brevity of this verse is striking — three verbs in rapid sequence: vayyifen ('he turned'), vayyir'eni ('he saw me'), vayyiqra elai ('he called to me'). Saul is portrayed as actively seeking help, scanning the battlefield for anyone who might assist him. The verb panah ('to turn') implies a deliberate rotation, not a casual glance. Saul's turning toward the Amalekite is a reversal of his entire career: the king of Israel, who was commanded to annihilate the Amalekites (1 Samuel 15), now turns to one for deliverance.
translator_notes: The three-verb sequence creates a cinematic progression: turn, see, call. Each verb advances the action with no wasted words. If the Amalekite's account is fabricated, this is skillful storytelling designed to make David believe he was summoned, not that he acted on his own initiative.

verses.text_hebrew: וַיֹּ֥אמֶר לִ֖י מִי־אָ֑תָּה וָאֹמַ֣ר אֵלָ֔יו עֲמָלֵקִ֖י אָנֹֽכִי׃
verses.text_kjv: And he said unto me, Who art thou? And I answered him, I am an Amalekite.
verses.rendering: He asked me, 'Who are you?' and I answered him, 'I am an Amalekite.'
verses.expanded_rendering: Saul's question mi attah ('who are you?') and the answer Amaleqi anokhi ('an Amalekite am I') create one of the most devastating ironies in the Hebrew Bible. Saul lost his kingship precisely because he failed to complete the destruction of the Amalekites (1 Samuel 15). The prophet Samuel told him that because he spared Agag and the Amalekite livestock, the LORD had torn the kingdom from him. Now, in his final moments, it is an Amalekite who stands over him — a living representative of the disobedience that cost him everything. The identity the man announces is his death warrant, though he does not yet know it.
translator_notes: The emphatic pronoun anokhi ('I myself') rather than the shorter ani adds weight to the self-identification. The Amalekite declares his ethnicity without hesitation, apparently unaware of the lethal history between Saul and his people. Whether this is historical or fabricated, the narrator exploits the dramatic irony to its fullest.

verses.text_hebrew: וַיֹּ֣אמֶר אֵלַ֗י עֲמָד־נָ֤א עָלַי֙ וּמֹ֣תְתֵ֔נִי כִּ֥י אֲחָזַ֖נִי הַשָּׁבָ֑ץ כִּי־כָל־ע֥וֹד נַפְשִׁ֖י בִּֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: He said unto me again, Stand, I pray thee, upon me, and slay me: for anguish is come upon me, because my life is yet whole in me.
verses.rendering: He said to me, 'Stand over me and kill me, because the death agony has seized me — yet my life is still in me.'
verses.expanded_rendering: Saul's command amad na alai u-motteteni ('stand over me and put me to death') is a request for a mercy killing. The verb mot in the Polel (motteteni) is an intensive causative — 'cause me to die completely.' The reason given is ki achazani ha-shavats ('because the shavats has seized me'). The word shavats is rare and debated: it may mean 'dizziness,' 'anguish,' 'cramping,' or 'the death convulsion.' Whatever the precise medical meaning, it describes a state between life and death — Saul is mortally wounded but not yet dead, trapped in prolonged agony. The final clause ki khol od nafshi bi ('because all yet my life is in me') is paradoxical: his life persists even as death overwhelms him. He is asking to be released from the space between living and dying.
translator_notes: The word shavats occurs only here in the Hebrew Bible, making its precise meaning uncertain. The Septuagint renders it as skotos ('darkness'), suggesting unconsciousness or dimming vision. Targum Jonathan reads 'trembling.' We render it as 'death agony' to capture the sense of a terminal but prolonged state of suffering.

The phrase kol od nafshi bi ('all my life is still in me') emphasizes that Saul is fully conscious and aware of his condition. This is not a quick death — it is drawn out, and Saul's request reflects the horror of being alive enough to suffer but too wounded to act.

verses.text_hebrew: וָאֶעֱמֹ֤ד עָלָיו֙ וַאֲמֹ֣תְתֵ֔הוּ כִּ֣י יָדַ֔עְתִּי כִּ֛י לֹ֥א יִחְיֶ֖ה אַחֲרֵ֣י נִפְל֑וֹ וָאֶקַּ֞ח הַנֵּ֣זֶר ׀ אֲשֶׁ֣ר עַל־רֹאשׁ֗וֹ וְאֶצְעָדָה֙ אֲשֶׁ֣ר עַל־זְרֹע֔וֹ וָאֲבִיאֵ֥ם אֶל־אֲדֹנִ֖י הֵֽנָּה׃
verses.text_kjv: So I stood upon him, and slew him, because I was sure that he could not live after that he was fallen: and I took the crown that was upon his head, and the bracelet that was on his arm, and have brought them hither unto my lord.
verses.rendering: So I stood over him and killed him, because I knew he could not survive after he had fallen. Then I took the crown from his head and the armband from his arm and brought them here to my lord.
translator_notes: The nezer is distinct from a full crown (atarah) — it is a diadem or band signifying consecrated authority, closely linked to the concept of being set apart (nazir). Saul's nezer represents his anointed status. The ets'adah ('armband') was a marker of military rank or royal status. Together these items function as regalia — proof of identity and proof of the messenger's claim.

The phrase el adoni hennah ('to my lord, here') positions David as Saul's successor in the messenger's own calculus. He has read the political situation — David is the obvious heir — and is attempting to position himself as the man who facilitated the transition. The irony is that this political calculation will cost him his life.

key_terms.semantic_range: diadem, consecration, crown, Nazirite dedication, separation
key_terms.note: The nezer is the visible mark of consecrated authority. It shares its root with nazir ('one set apart') and nezer in the sense of Nazirite vow (Numbers 6:7). When placed on the king's head, it signifies that the wearer is set apart for sovereign rule under God. The Amalekite's removal of the nezer from Saul's head and delivery to David enacts — without authorization — the very transfer of kingship that God had decreed through Samuel.

verses.text_hebrew: וַיַּחֲזֵ֤ק דָּוִד֙ בִּבְגָדָ֔יו וַיִּקְרָעֵ֑ם וְגַ֥ם כׇּל־הָאֲנָשִׁ֖ים אֲשֶׁ֥ר אִתּֽוֹ׃
verses.text_kjv: Then David took hold on his clothes, and rent them; and likewise all the men that were with him:
verses.rendering: David seized his own garments and tore them, and so did all the men who were with him.
verses.expanded_rendering: The verb vayyachazek ('he seized, he gripped firmly') is intensive — David does not merely pluck at his clothing but grips it with force before tearing. The act of qeri'ah (tearing garments) is the prescribed physical response to news of death in ancient Israel (Genesis 37:34, Job 1:20). David's grief becomes communal: vegam kol ha-anashim asher itto ('and also all the men who were with him') follow suit. The entire camp enters into mourning. This is not private sorrow but a public, collective ritual of lament. The men with David are his band of outlaws and refugees — men who had fled from Saul's persecution — and yet they mourn Saul's death with full ceremonial gravity.
translator_notes: The verb chazaq in the Hiphil ('to seize, to grasp firmly') emphasizes the violence of the gesture. Garment-tearing in grief was not a gentle rip but a forceful act — the physical destruction of clothing externalized the internal shattering of grief. The communal tearing indicates that David's men follow his lead in honoring Saul, despite their history as fugitives from Saul's court.

verses.text_hebrew: וַֽיִּסְפְּדוּ֙ וַיִּבְכּ֔וּ וַיָּצֻ֖מוּ עַד־הָעָ֑רֶב עַל־שָׁא֞וּל וְעַל־יְהוֹנָתָ֣ן בְּנ֗וֹ וְעַל־עַ֤ם יְהוָה֙ וְעַל־בֵּ֣ית יִשְׂרָאֵ֔ל כִּ֥י נָפְל֖וּ בֶּחָֽרֶב׃
verses.text_kjv: And they mourned, and wept, and fasted until even, for Saul, and for Jonathan his son, and for the people of the LORD, and for the house of Israel; because they were fallen by the sword.
verses.rendering: They mourned, wept, and fasted until evening — for Saul, for his son Jonathan, for the people of the LORD, and for the house of Israel, because they had fallen by the sword.
verses.expanded_rendering: Three verbs of grief in sequence: vayyispedu ('they lamented' — the formal wailing of a funeral dirge), vayyivku ('they wept'), vayyatsumu ('they fasted'). The mourning extends until evening (ad ha-arev), marking a full day of grief. The objects of mourning expand outward in concentric circles: first Saul personally, then Jonathan, then am YHWH ('the people of the LORD' — the covenant community), and finally beit Yisra'el ('the house of Israel' — the national entity). David mourns not only two individuals but the collective disaster that has befallen the nation. The final clause ki nafelu becharev ('because they fell by the sword') uses the same verb naphal ('to fall') that will dominate the coming lament.
translator_notes: The phrase am YHWH ('the people of the LORD') is theologically loaded — David identifies the fallen soldiers not merely as Israelite troops but as the covenant people of God. Their death in battle is a disaster not only for the nation but for God's purposes. The distinction between am YHWH and beit Yisra'el may be intentional: the first is a theological designation (the covenant people), the second is a political one (the national household).

Fasting until evening was a standard mourning practice (Judges 20:26, 2 Samuel 3:35). The three actions — lamenting, weeping, fasting — form a complete ritual of grief, performed communally and lasting the full day.

verses.text_hebrew: וַיֹּ֣אמֶר דָּוִ֗ד אֶל־הַנַּ֙עַר֙ הַמַּגִּ֣יד ל֔וֹ אֵ֥י מִזֶּ֖ה אָ֑תָּה וַיֹּ֕אמֶר בֶּן־אִ֥ישׁ גֵּר־עֲמָלֵקִ֖י אָנֹֽכִי׃
verses.text_kjv: And David said unto the young man that told him, Whence art thou? And he answered, I am the son of a stranger, an Amalekite.
verses.rendering: David said to the young man who had brought the report, "Where are you from?" He answered, "I am the son of an Amalekite resident alien."
translator_notes: The term ger is one of the most significant social categories in the Torah — the resident alien who dwells among the people of Israel and is entitled to protection, justice, and inclusion in certain religious observances. By identifying himself as a ger's son, the Amalekite claims legal standing within the Israelite social order. David will use this very status against him in verse 16: as a resident within Israel, the man should have known better than to strike the LORD's anointed.

verses.text_hebrew: וַיֹּ֥אמֶר אֵלָ֖יו דָּוִ֑ד אֵ֚יךְ לֹ֣א יָרֵ֔אתָ לִשְׁלֹ֙חַ֙ יָֽדְךָ֔ לְשַׁחֵ֖ת אֶת־מְשִׁ֥יחַ יְהוָֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: And David said unto him, How wast thou not afraid to stretch forth thine hand to destroy the LORD's anointed?
verses.rendering: David said to him, "How is it that you were not afraid to reach out your hand and destroy the anointed of the LORD?"
verses.expanded_rendering: David's question is a thunderbolt. The verb yareta ('you feared') with the negative lo creates an expression of astonishment: 'How did you not fear?' The phrase lishloch yadekha ('to stretch out your hand') is the standard idiom for violent action against a person — the same phrase David used when refusing to harm Saul himself (1 Samuel 24:6, 26:9). The object of the violence is meshiach YHWH ('the anointed of the LORD'), the most theologically charged designation for the king in the Hebrew Bible. The mashiach is the one upon whom sacred oil has been poured, setting him apart as God's designated ruler. To strike the mashiach is to strike against God's own choice. David, who twice spared Saul's life precisely because of this principle, now demands an accounting from the man who claims to have violated it.
translator_notes: The phrase meshiach YHWH ('the LORD's anointed') is the theological heart of this exchange. The mashiach is untouchable — not because of personal merit but because of divine designation. David's entire fugitive career was governed by this conviction: even when Saul was trying to kill him, David would not raise his hand against the one God had anointed. The Amalekite's claim to have killed the mashiach places him in direct violation of the principle that David holds most sacred.

key_terms.semantic_range: anointed one, consecrated ruler, the LORD's chosen king, messiah
key_terms.note: The mashiach is literally 'the smeared one' — the person upon whom sacred oil has been poured as a sign of divine selection for kingship or priesthood. In the Samuel narratives, this term creates a protective aura around the king: whoever harms the mashiach YHWH acts against God's own appointment. David's theology of the mashiach will define his kingship and become the seedbed for later messianic expectation.

verses.text_hebrew: וַיִּקְרָ֣א דָוִ֗ד לְאַחַד֙ מֵֽהַנְּעָרִ֔ים וַיֹּ֖אמֶר גַּ֣שׁ פְּגַע־בּ֑וֹ וַיַּכֵּ֖הוּ וַיָּמֹֽת׃
verses.text_kjv: And David called one of the young men, and said, Go near, and fall upon him. And he smote him that he died.
verses.rendering: David summoned one of the young men and said, "Go, strike him down." The man struck him, and he died.
verses.expanded_rendering: David's command is terse and decisive: gash pega bo ('approach, strike him'). The verb paga in this context means to strike fatally — the same verb used for judicial execution. There is no trial, no deliberation, no appeal. David acts as a sovereign exercising the power of life and death, even though he does not yet hold the throne. The execution is immediate: vayyakkehu vayyamot ('he struck him and he died'). Two verbs, two actions, one death. The Amalekite who expected reward for delivering royal regalia receives instead the punishment of a regicide.
translator_notes: The verb paga ('to encounter, to strike') in the imperative carries the force of an execution order. It is the same verb used for the killing of the priests at Nob (1 Samuel 22:18), where Doeg the Edomite was willing to 'strike' them when Saul's own servants would not. The economy of the narration — a single sentence for the execution — mirrors the swift finality of the act.

verses.text_hebrew: וַיֹּ֤אמֶר אֵלָיו֙ דָּוִ֔ד דָּמְךָ֖ עַל־רֹאשֶׁ֑ךָ כִּ֣י פִ֗יךָ עָנָ֤ה בְךָ֙ לֵאמֹ֔ר אָנֹכִ֥י מֹתַ֖תִּי אֶת־מְשִׁ֥יחַ יְהוָֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: And David said unto him, Thy blood be upon thy own head; for thy mouth hath testified against thee, saying, I have slain the LORD's anointed.
verses.rendering: David said to him, "Your blood is on your own head, because your own mouth testified against you when you said, 'I killed the anointed of the LORD.'"
verses.expanded_rendering: David pronounces the legal verdict after the execution: damekha al roshekha ('your blood is upon your own head'). This is a formal declaration of self-incurred guilt — the man's death is his own responsibility, not David's. The legal reasoning follows: ki fikha anah vekha ('because your mouth answered against you'). The verb anah ('to answer, to testify') is courtroom language — the man's own confession serves as witness testimony. David does not investigate whether the Amalekite's account is true or fabricated. The claim itself is the crime. To declare 'I killed the anointed of the LORD' is to confess a capital offense regardless of whether it actually happened. The phrase meshiach YHWH appears again, underscoring that the sanctity of the anointed is the operative legal principle.
translator_notes: The formula 'your blood is on your own head' appears throughout the Hebrew Bible as a declaration that the executed person bears responsibility for their own death (Joshua 2:19, Ezekiel 33:4). David uses it here to establish that the execution is just — the Amalekite's own testimony condemned him.

The repetition of meshiach YHWH from verse 14 frames the entire exchange: David's question ('how were you not afraid to destroy the LORD's anointed?') and his verdict ('you said you killed the LORD's anointed') form an inclusio around the execution.

key_terms.semantic_range: anointed one, consecrated ruler, the LORD's chosen king, messiah
key_terms.note: Second occurrence in this chapter (see v. 14). David's verdict rests entirely on this concept: the mashiach possesses a sacred inviolability that transcends personal worthiness. Even a dead mashiach retains this status — killing the LORD's anointed after the fact is still a capital offense.

verses.text_hebrew: וַיְקֹנֵ֣ן דָּוִ֔ד אֶת־הַקִּינָ֖ה הַזֹּ֑את עַל־שָׁא֖וּל וְעַל־יְהוֹנָתָ֥ן בְּנֽוֹ׃
verses.text_kjv: And David lamented with this lamentation over Saul and over Jonathan his son:
verses.rendering: David chanted this lament over Saul and over his son Jonathan:
translator_notes: The qinah is a recognized poetic genre in the Hebrew Bible, characterized by its distinctive 3:2 'limping meter' — a longer line followed by a shorter one, creating a rhythmic sense of falling or stumbling that mirrors the emotional content of grief. Jeremiah, Ezekiel, and the book of Lamentations all employ qinah meter. David's lament over Saul and Jonathan is one of the earliest and finest examples of the form.

key_terms.semantic_range: dirge, funeral song, elegy, lamentation, death-poem
key_terms.note: The qinah is a formal poetic genre reserved for mourning the dead. Its distinctive meter (3 beats followed by 2 beats in each line) creates a 'limping' rhythm — the line stumbles and falls short, enacting the collapse of life in the very structure of the verse. David's qinah over Saul and Jonathan is the Song of the Bow, one of the most celebrated poems in the Hebrew Bible.

verses.text_hebrew: וַיֹּ֕אמֶר לְלַמֵּ֥ד בְּנֵי־יְהוּדָ֖ה קָ֑שֶׁת הִנֵּ֥ה כְתוּבָ֖ה עַל־סֵ֥פֶר הַיָּשָֽׁר׃
verses.text_kjv: Also he bade them teach the children of Judah the use of the bow: behold, it is written in the book of Jasher:
verses.rendering: He ordered that the sons of Judah be taught 'The Bow.' It is recorded in the Book of Jashar:
verses.expanded_rendering: This verse is syntactically difficult: vayyomer lelammed benei Yehudah qashet ('he said to teach the sons of Judah — bow'). The word qeshet ('bow') stands somewhat abruptly at the end of the clause. Most interpreters understand it as the title of the poem — 'The Bow' — named either for Jonathan's skill with the bow (v. 22) or because the poem itself was to be memorized as a kind of martial curriculum. David commands that the lament be taught (lammed) to the tribe of Judah specifically — his own tribe, the people he will soon rule. The reference to the Book of Jashar (sefer ha-yashar, 'the Book of the Upright') points to an ancient anthology of Hebrew heroic poetry, now lost, also cited in Joshua 10:13. The lament was preserved in written form before being incorporated into the Samuel narrative.
translator_notes: The word qeshet ('bow') as a poem title is attested in the Masoretic text but absent from some Septuagint manuscripts. Some scholars emend it or treat it as a marginal gloss. We retain it as the title following the MT, understanding it as David's designation for the lament — 'The Bow' — likely honoring Jonathan's weapon of choice.

The Book of Jashar (sefer ha-yashar) is mentioned only here and in Joshua 10:13 (and possibly 1 Kings 8:53 in the LXX). It was evidently a collection of ancient poems celebrating Israel's heroes. Its loss means that the biblical quotations from it are our only surviving fragments.

key_terms.note: Here qeshet functions as the title of David's lament. The bow was Jonathan's signature weapon — in 1 Samuel 18:4 he gave his bow to David as a covenant gift, and in verse 22 of this lament David will celebrate the bow that 'never turned back.' The title memorializes Jonathan's martial skill and, by extension, the covenant bond between the two warriors.

verses.text_hebrew: הַצְּבִ֖י יִשְׂרָאֵ֑ל עַל־בָּמוֹתֶ֣יךָ חָלָ֔ל אֵ֖יךְ נָפְל֥וּ גִבּוֹרִֽים׃
verses.text_kjv: The beauty of Israel is slain upon thy high places: how are the mighty fallen!
verses.rendering:
The splendor of Israel lies slain on your heights.
How the mighty have fallen!
verses.expanded_rendering: The lament opens with its thesis statement. Ha-tsevi Yisra'el ('the beauty/glory/gazelle of Israel') is deliberately ambiguous — tsevi can mean 'beauty,' 'glory,' 'honor,' or 'gazelle' (a symbol of swiftness and grace). It refers to the fallen warriors collectively, or to Saul and Jonathan specifically, as the embodiment of Israel's honor. The word bamotekha ('your high places') refers to the heights of Gilboa where the battle was fought, but bamot also carries associations with sacred high places of worship — the heights where Israel met God are now the heights where Israel's glory lies dead. The word chalal ('pierced, slain') is the same term used in 1 Samuel 31:1 for the fallen on Gilboa. The refrain eikh nafelu gibborim ('how the mighty have fallen') introduces the poem's structural anchor — it will return in verses 25 and 27, framing the entire lament. The word gibborim ('mighty ones, warriors, heroes') designates men of extraordinary martial valor.
translator_notes: The opening word ha-tsevi has been rendered many ways: 'the beauty' (KJV), 'the glory,' 'the gazelle,' 'thy glory, O Israel.' We chose 'the splendor' to capture both the aesthetic and the honorific senses without limiting it to a single referent. The word functions as a title for those who have fallen — they were Israel's splendor, and now that splendor is dead.

The refrain eikh nafelu gibborim is the poem's signature line. The interrogative eikh ('how!') is not a question expecting an answer but an exclamation of disbelief and grief — 'How is it possible that the mighty have fallen!' The qinah meter is audible here: three beats (eikh nafelu gibborim) followed by the silence of what cannot be said.

verses.text_hebrew: אַל־תַּגִּ֣ידוּ בְגַ֔ת אַֽל־תְּבַשְּׂר֖וּ בְּחוּצֹ֣ת אַשְׁקְל֑וֹן פֶּן־תִּשְׂמַ֙חְנָה֙ בְּנ֣וֹת פְּלִשְׁתִּ֔ים פֶּֽן־תַּעֲלֹ֖זְנָה בְּנ֥וֹת הָעֲרֵלִֽים׃
verses.text_kjv: Tell it not in Gath, publish it not in the streets of Askelon; lest the daughters of the Philistines rejoice, lest the daughters of the uncircumcised triumph.
verses.rendering:
Do not tell it in Gath,
do not proclaim it in the streets of Ashkelon —
lest the daughters of the Philistines rejoice,
lest the daughters of the uncircumcised celebrate.
verses.expanded_rendering: David's first command is an impossible wish: al taggidu ve-Gat ('do not tell it in Gath'). Gath was the Philistine city where David himself had taken refuge (1 Samuel 21:10, 27:2-4) — he knows its streets, its people, its capacity for triumph over fallen Israel. The parallel line names Ashkelon, another of the five Philistine cities. The verb tevasseru ('proclaim, bring good news') is bitterly ironic — the root basar is the word for 'good tidings,' and David is saying: do not let this become good news in enemy territory. The motivation clause reveals the deepest wound: pen tismachnah benot Pelishtim ('lest the daughters of the Philistines rejoice'). The 'daughters' are the women who sing victory songs in the streets — the same cultural practice that produced the song 'Saul has struck his thousands, and David his ten thousands' (1 Samuel 18:7). Now David fears the Philistine women will compose their own victory song over Saul's body.
translator_notes: The phrase al taggidu ve-Gat has become proverbial in Hebrew — it is the idiom for suppressing shameful news. The parallelism between Gath and Ashkelon follows standard Hebrew poetic convention of pairing cities. The 'daughters of the uncircumcised' (benot ha-arelim) echoes Saul's final words in 1 Samuel 31:4, binding the lament to the death scene.

The verb alaz ('to exult, to celebrate with triumph') in the second motivation clause intensifies beyond the simple simchah ('joy') of the first. David imagines escalating Philistine celebration — first rejoicing, then exultation. The poet's craft is visible in the building parallelism.

verses.text_hebrew: הָרֵ֣י בַגִּלְבֹּ֗עַ אַל־טַ֧ל וְאַל־מָטָ֛ר עֲלֵיכֶ֖ם וּשְׂדֵ֣י תְרוּמֹ֑ת כִּ֣י שָׁ֗ם נִגְעַל֙ מָגֵ֣ן גִּבּוֹרִ֔ים מָגֵ֣ן שָׁא֔וּל בְּלִ֖י מָשִׁ֥יחַ בַּשָּֽׁמֶן׃
verses.text_kjv: Ye mountains of Gilboa, let there be no dew, neither let there be rain, upon you, nor fields of offerings: for there the shield of the mighty is vilely cast away, the shield of Saul, as though he had not been anointed with oil.
verses.rendering:
Mountains of Gilboa — let no dew fall on you,
no rain upon your terraced fields!
For there the shield of the mighty was defiled,
the shield of Saul — no longer rubbed with oil.
translator_notes: The curse on Gilboa's fertility echoes ancient Near Eastern curses on battle sites — the earth that drank warriors' blood is condemned to perpetual barrenness. The double meaning of mashiach (anointed king / oiled shield) is one of the most celebrated wordplays in Hebrew poetry. David does not say directly that Saul the anointed one has been rejected; he says the shield is no longer oiled. The reader hears both meanings simultaneously.

The phrase sedei terumot is difficult. Terumot usually means 'heave offerings' or 'contributions' (a priestly term), but here it may mean 'uplifted fields' — terraced agricultural land. Some emend to sedei re'umot ('fields of deception') or sedei terumah ('fields of the offering'). We render 'terraced fields' as the most geographically concrete reading.

verses.text_hebrew: מִדַּ֣ם חֲלָלִ֗ים מֵחֵ֙לֶב֙ גִּבּוֹרִ֔ים קֶ֚שֶׁת יְה֣וֹנָתָ֔ן לֹ֥א נָשׂ֖וֹג אָח֑וֹר וְחֶ֣רֶב שָׁא֔וּל לֹ֥א תָשׁ֖וּב רֵיקָֽם׃
verses.text_kjv: From the blood of the slain, from the fat of the mighty, the bow of Jonathan turned not back, and the sword of Saul returned not empty.
verses.rendering:
From the blood of the slain,
from the flesh of the mighty,
the bow of Jonathan never turned back,
and the sword of Saul never returned empty.
translator_notes: The word chelev is usually rendered 'fat,' but in poetic contexts it can mean 'the best part, the richest portion.' We chose 'flesh' to avoid the modern connotation of adipose tissue and to convey the sense of bodily substance. The phrase lo nasog achor ('did not draw back') uses the military language of retreat — Jonathan's bow is personified as a warrior that never retreated from the front line.

The pairing of Jonathan's bow with Saul's sword matches each man to his signature weapon. Jonathan was an archer (1 Samuel 20:20-22, 35-40), and the bow was the weapon he gave David as a covenant gift (1 Samuel 18:4). Saul was associated with the sword — and ultimately fell on one (1 Samuel 31:4).

verses.text_hebrew: שָׁא֣וּל וִיהוֹנָתָ֗ן הַנֶּאֱהָבִ֤ים וְהַנְּעִימִם֙ בְּחַיֵּיהֶ֔ם וּבְמוֹתָ֖ם לֹ֣א נִפְרָ֑דוּ מִנְּשָׁרִ֣ים קַ֔לּוּ מֵאֲרָי֖וֹת גָּבֵֽרוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: Saul and Jonathan were lovely and pleasant in their lives, and in their death they were not divided: they were swifter than eagles, they were stronger than lions.
verses.rendering:
Saul and Jonathan — beloved and cherished in life,
and in death they were not divided.
They were swifter than eagles,
stronger than lions.
translator_notes: The phrase u-ve-motam lo nifradu ('in their death they were not divided') may also carry a polemical edge: despite the political rupture between Saul and Jonathan over David's future (1 Samuel 20:30-34), David insists that father and son died united. The poet reclaims the relationship from its political fractures.

The nesher is most likely the griffon vulture (Gyps fulvus), not the modern bald eagle — the largest raptor in the Levant, known for soaring at immense heights and diving with devastating speed. The ari is the now-extinct Asiatic lion (Panthera leo persica), which inhabited the Jordan Valley and hill country into the medieval period.

verses.text_hebrew: בְּנוֹת֙ יִשְׂרָאֵ֔ל אֶל־שָׁא֖וּל בְּכֶ֑ינָה הַמַּלְבִּשְׁכֶ֤ם שָׁנִי֙ עִם־עֲדָנִ֔ים הַֽמַּעֲלֶ֛ה עֲדִ֥י זָהָ֖ב עַל־לְבוּשְׁכֶֽן׃
verses.text_kjv: Ye daughters of Israel, weep over Saul, who clothed you in scarlet, with other delights, who put on ornaments of gold upon your apparel.
verses.rendering:
Daughters of Israel, weep over Saul,
who clothed you in scarlet and fine garments,
who adorned your clothing with gold ornaments.
verses.expanded_rendering: David turns from the warriors to the women, commanding the daughters of Israel (benot Yisra'el) to weep. This is a deliberate counterpoint to verse 20, where he feared the daughters of the Philistines would rejoice. The Philistine women celebrate; the Israelite women must mourn. The reason for mourning is concrete and material: Saul brought them prosperity. The verb hammalbishkhem ('the one who clothed you') credits Saul with providing luxury goods — shani ('scarlet' — the expensive crimson dye extracted from kermes insects) and adanim ('fine things, delicacies, luxuries'). The second line intensifies: hamma'aleh adi zahav al levushkhen ('the one who placed gold ornaments upon your garments'). David's portrait of Saul here is not of a warrior-king but of a provider-king — the ruler whose military success translated into economic abundance for his people. The gold and scarlet are the spoils of war redistributed to the nation.
translator_notes: The appeal to women as professional mourners is consistent with Israelite practice — women led formal keening and lamentation (Jeremiah 9:17-20). But David's address is broader: all the daughters of Israel are to mourn because all benefited from Saul's reign. The word shani ('scarlet') denotes a luxury textile associated with wealth and royalty (Proverbs 31:21). The adanim ('delights') may include fine fabrics, jewelry, or other imported goods.

The emphasis on material prosperity is not shallow. In the ancient world, a king's ability to provide luxury goods to his people was the tangible proof of his military and economic success. David is saying: Saul enriched you — honor him with your grief.

verses.text_hebrew: אֵ֚יךְ נָפְל֣וּ גִבּוֹרִ֔ים בְּת֖וֹךְ הַמִּלְחָמָ֑ה יְה֣וֹנָתָ֔ן עַל־בָּמוֹתֶ֖יךָ חָלָֽל׃
verses.text_kjv: How are the mighty fallen in the midst of the battle! O Jonathan, thou wast slain in thine high places.
verses.rendering:
How the mighty have fallen in the thick of battle!
Jonathan lies slain on your heights.
verses.expanded_rendering: The refrain returns: eikh nafelu gibborim ('how the mighty have fallen'), now expanded with betokh hammilchamah ('in the midst of the battle'). The mighty did not fall in retreat or in ambush but in the very center of the fighting — they fell facing their enemies. Then the lament narrows from the collective gibborim to the personal: Yehonatan al bamotekha chalal ('Jonathan — upon your heights, slain'). The second-person address ('your heights') shifts the poem from public eulogy to intimate address. David is no longer speaking about Jonathan to the daughters of Israel; he is speaking to the land itself, or to the dead. The transition marks the beginning of the poem's most personal section — the passage from king to friend.
translator_notes: The shift from third-person plural (the mighty) to second-person singular (your heights) is a characteristic qinah technique — the poet moves from public grief to private address, collapsing the distance between the mourner and the dead. The word bamotekha ('your heights') echoes verse 19, creating a structural echo: the poem's opening and closing movements both invoke the heights where the blood was shed.

verses.text_hebrew: צַר־לִ֣י עָלֶ֗יךָ אָחִי֙ יְה֣וֹנָתָ֔ן נָעַ֥מְתָּ לִּ֖י מְאֹ֑ד נִפְלְאַ֤תָה אַהֲבָֽתְךָ֙ לִ֔י מֵאַהֲבַ֖ת נָשִֽׁים׃
verses.text_kjv: I am distressed for thee, my brother Jonathan: very pleasant hast thou been unto me: thy love to me was wonderful, passing the love of women.
verses.rendering:
I am in anguish over you, my brother Jonathan.
You were so dear to me.
Your love for me was extraordinary —
surpassing the love of women.
verses.expanded_rendering: David's address to Jonathan is the most intimate passage in the Hebrew Bible between two men. The opening tsar li alekha ('I am in distress over you') uses tsar, meaning 'narrow, constricted, anguished' — David feels the walls closing in, his world shrinking with the loss. The word achi ('my brother') is a covenant term, not a biological one — David and Jonathan are brothers by oath, not by blood (1 Samuel 18:1-4, 20:17). The verb na'amta ('you were pleasant, you were dear') shares its root with na'im from verse 23, connecting Jonathan's public reputation to David's private experience of him. The climactic declaration — nifl'atah ahavatekha li me'ahavat nashim ('your love was extraordinary to me, beyond the love of women') — uses the verb pala ('to be wonderful, to be beyond ordinary experience'), the same root used for God's wonders (nifla'ot). Jonathan's ahavah (love, loyalty, covenantal devotion) exceeded the most intimate human bond David knew. The word ahavah encompasses covenantal loyalty, deep friendship, political allegiance, and personal devotion — the full semantic range of committed love in Hebrew. David is not comparing types of affection but declaring that no human relationship he has experienced matched the depth of Jonathan's faithfulness.
translator_notes: The phrase me'ahavat nashim ('beyond the love of women') has generated extensive scholarly discussion. In context, ahavah is the covenant loyalty that bound David and Jonathan from 1 Samuel 18:1 onward — a love that survived Saul's attempts to kill David, Jonathan's renunciation of his own dynastic claim, and years of separation. David declares that this covenantal bond surpassed every other intimate relationship in his life. The word ahavah in the Hebrew Bible ranges from romantic desire (Song of Songs) to political treaty loyalty (1 Kings 5:1) to God's covenant love for Israel (Deuteronomy 7:8). Here it functions in its broadest and deepest sense: total, sacrificial commitment.

The word nifl'atah ('it was extraordinary, it was wonderful') belongs to the vocabulary of divine action — the nifla'ot are God's wonders, the acts that exceed human comprehension (Exodus 15:11, Psalm 77:11). David applies this theological vocabulary to a human relationship, elevating Jonathan's love to the level of the miraculous.

key_terms.semantic_range: love, covenantal loyalty, devotion, deep affection, political allegiance, committed faithfulness
key_terms.note: Ahavah is the most comprehensive word for committed love in Hebrew. It is used for God's love for Israel (Deuteronomy 7:8), a husband's love for his wife (Genesis 29:20), a friend's love for a friend (1 Samuel 18:1), and a treaty partner's loyalty (1 Kings 5:1). David's declaration that Jonathan's ahavah surpassed the ahavah of women places their covenant bond at the apex of David's relational experience. The word refuses to be confined to a single modern category — it encompasses the full range of faithful, self-giving human connection.

verses.text_hebrew: אֵ֚יךְ נָפְל֣וּ גִבּוֹרִ֔ים וַיֹּאבְד֖וּ כְּלֵ֥י מִלְחָמָֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: How are the mighty fallen, and the weapons of war perished!
verses.rendering:
How the mighty have fallen,
and the weapons of war have perished!
verses.expanded_rendering: The lament closes with its refrain for the third and final time: eikh nafelu gibborim ('how the mighty have fallen'). But the final line adds a new element: vayyovedu kelei milchamah ('and the weapons of war have perished'). The word kelei ('vessels, instruments, weapons') is the same term used for a warrior's full equipment — armor, weapons, and implements. The verb avad ('to perish, to be destroyed, to be lost') is absolute: the instruments of war have not merely been captured or discarded but have ceased to exist. On one level, David mourns the literal loss of weapons and armor (stripped by the Philistines in 1 Samuel 31:9). On a deeper level, kelei milchamah ('weapons of war') is a metaphor for the warriors themselves — Saul and Jonathan were Israel's weapons, and now those weapons are gone. The poem ends not with consolation or hope but with the bare fact of loss. There is no theology, no promise of restoration, no afterlife comfort. The mighty have fallen. The weapons have perished. Silence.
translator_notes: The three occurrences of the refrain (vv. 19, 25, 27) create the structural architecture of the poem: opening statement, central development, final summation. The addition of vayyovedu kelei milchamah in the closing refrain prevents the ending from being a mere repetition — it adds the finality of utter destruction. The poem that began with 'the splendor of Israel' ends with 'weapons of war' — the full arc from glory to wreckage.

The absence of any mention of God in the entire lament (vv. 19-27) is remarkable. David makes no appeal to divine justice, offers no theological explanation for the disaster, and provides no eschatological hope. This is a poem of pure human grief, uttered by a man who elsewhere attributes everything to God but here speaks only of what has been lost on earth. The silence about God may itself be the poem's deepest theological statement: some losses are too large for easy piety.
